{
 "cells": [
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "dbec38ac-c172-41ea-a493-d7cf3e4d3cbf",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# Modeling BLS signal from a single magnetic layer using the reciprocity theorem #2"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "abf9230b-61a8-4c75-9956-6e296342708f",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "This example shows how SpinWaveToolkit (SWT) can be used to calculate the BLS signal using the reciprocity theorem and laser electric fields in reciprocal space.\n",
    "\n",
    "The only difference to example #1 is the use of laser electric fields in reciprocal space (compared to electric fields in real space in example #1). The use of reciprocal-space fields results in potentially faster calculations of the final BLS spectra. They should produce the same results (when rescaled), but it is not guaranteed (especially when calculating coherent magnon signal, which is not the case in this example). Therefore, make sure to (briefly) compare results of the two approaches before coming to conclusions.\n",
    "\n",
    "Source publication: Krčma et al., *Science Advances* **11**, eady8833 (2025). DOI: [10.1126/sciadv.ady8833](https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.ady8833)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "1c356c60-3791-43ab-94c2-4ad1f286740a",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### 1. Import modules and define parameters"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 1,
   "id": "ea1e8729-2228-4f49-862d-5d76c35a4b4f",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "import numpy as np\n",
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "import SpinWaveToolkit as SWT"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 2,
   "id": "5b0f15b7-b71e-4ac0-a128-1b694d7380b1",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# Define parameters\n",
    "Bext = 50e-3            # (T) external field\n",
    "theta = np.pi / 2       # (rad) out-of-plane angle (fixed)\n",
    "d_layer = 30e-9         # (m) magnetic layer thickness\n",
    "material = SWT.NiFe     # Material\n",
    "Nf_common = 101         # number of frequency points for Bloch function\n",
    "fmin, fmax = 1e9, 35e9  # (Hz) frequency range for Bloch functions\n",
    "\n",
    "NA = 0.75               # Numerical Aperture (NA) for the lens\n",
    "\n",
    "# Define the Kx,Ky grid limits and resolution.\n",
    "k_min = 1e-6            # (rad/m) minimum k (avoid zero if necessary)\n",
    "k_max = 20e6            # (rad/m) maximum k (as in original kxi range)\n",
    "dk = 0.2e6\n",
    "Nk = int(round((k_max-k_min)/dk))  # resolution in Kx and Ky"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "46c8cc20-f09a-4257-a622-223244386d84",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### 2. Calculate the dynamic susceptibility"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "f6e3e9fc",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Now, the dynamic susceptibility of spin waves needs to be calculated. We do this by calculating the respective spin-wave Bloch functions and from those we construct the magneto-optical dynamic susceptibility tensor.\n",
    "\n",
    "When building the dynamic magnetization components `mx`, `my`, `mz` from Bloch functions, the direction of static magnetization has to comply with the `phi0` and `theta` parameters (e.g. here $\\vec{M}_0\\parallel y$, so `my==0`). It is recommended to stick to magnetization directions parallel with the main coordinate axes, as calculation of the dynamic components is not so trivial in the general case. Similarly, it is easier to rotate the electric fields than to rotate the susceptibility tensor, and therefore the magneto-optical tensors are implemented only for magnetization direction parallel to x, y, or z.\n",
    "\n",
    "In this simple case, we assume only linear part of the MO susceptibility tensor and therefore we use `SWT.bls.susceptibilities.mo_linear()` to construct it. However, SWT offers even quadratic MO calculations. See the documentation of the `susceptibilities` submodule for more details."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 3,
   "id": "09836b58-cddd-4265-9cb7-114f213204c7",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Preparing dynamic susceptibility tensor...\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "print(\"Preparing dynamic susceptibility tensor...\")\n",
    "\n",
    "# Setup k-space grid\n",
    "kx_grid = np.linspace(-k_max, k_max, Nk)\n",
    "ky_grid = np.linspace(-k_max, k_max, Nk)\n",
    "KX, KY = np.meshgrid(kx_grid, ky_grid, indexing=\"ij\")\n",
    "\n",
    "# k-magnitude and propagation angle\n",
    "phi0 = np.pi / 2\n",
    "kxi = np.sqrt(KX**2 + KY**2)\n",
    "phi = SWT.wrapAngle(np.arctan2(KY, KX) + phi0)\n",
    "phi[kxi <= 1e-12] = 0.0  # avoid undefined angle at k=0\n",
    "\n",
    "# Flatten for vectorized evaluation\n",
    "kxi_fl = kxi.flatten()\n",
    "phi_fl = phi.flatten()\n",
    "\n",
    "# Allocate Bloch function storage\n",
    "Bloch2D = np.zeros((Nf_common, Nk, Nk), dtype=complex)\n",
    "w_common = np.linspace(2 * np.pi * fmin, 2 * np.pi * fmax, Nf_common)  # common frequency axis\n",
    "\n",
    "# ...\n",
    "# Create a SingleLayer model for the current kxi and phi.\n",
    "# Note: We pass kxi and phi as flattened arrays of same shape.\n",
    "model = SWT.SingleLayer(Bext=Bext, kxi=kxi_fl, theta=theta,\n",
    "                        phi=phi_fl, d=d_layer, material=material)\n",
    "# Compute the Bloch functions for n=0,1,2.\n",
    "# The returned w has shape (Nf_common,) and bf has shape (Nf_common, len(kxi))\n",
    "w0, bf0 = model.GetBlochFunction(n=0, Nf=Nf_common)\n",
    "w1, bf1 = model.GetBlochFunction(n=1, Nf=Nf_common)\n",
    "# reshape Bloch functions to match the Kx,Ky grid shape\n",
    "bf0 = bf0.reshape((Nf_common, Nk, Nk))\n",
    "bf1 = bf1.reshape((Nf_common, Nk, Nk))\n",
    "# Loop over all grid points in the Kx,Ky plane.\n",
    "for i in range(Nk):\n",
    "    for j in range(Nk):\n",
    "        # Interpolate the Bloch functions to the common frequency axis.\n",
    "        bf0_interp = np.interp(w_common, w0, bf0[:, i, j], left=0, right=0)\n",
    "        bf1_interp = np.interp(w_common, w1, bf1[:, i, j], left=0, right=0)\n",
    "\n",
    "        # Sum Bloch functions for n=0,1,2\n",
    "        Bloch2D[:, i, j] = bf0_interp + bf1_interp\n",
    "\n",
    "# Build susceptibility tensor χ\n",
    "mx = Bloch2D\n",
    "my = np.zeros_like(Bloch2D)\n",
    "mz = Bloch2D * -1j\n",
    "chi = SWT.bls.susceptibilities.mo_linear([mx, my, mz])"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "c6601710-dd78-4102-8b78-5d86397bc46a",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### 3. Prepare the electric fields"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "fa7ff2fd-8710-454b-9a66-5a646ba7c990",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Now, the electric fields need to be calculated. Ei corresponds to the driving field (incident laser) and the Ej to the virtual source. In accordance with our BLS detection scheme, they are calculated with orthogonal polarizations. [Note that this is the usual condition to maximize SW signal, but the model can calculate signal from an arbitrary polarization direction of the fields, allowing for polarization analysis studies, such as Szulc et al. [arXiv:2602.15760 (2026)](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2602.15760).] The electric fields can either be obtained from SWT or numerically (FDTD solvers).\n",
    "\n",
    "To get the field from SWT, the `ObjectiveLens` class can be used. To rotate the polarization, you can use the `rotate_field()` helper function or set the polarization angle (if available by the respective method of `ObjectiveLens`)."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 4,
   "id": "a346df17-42e7-414f-ab76-5cab3703fcab",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Preparing focal field...\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "print(\"Preparing focal field...\")\n",
    "objective = SWT.bls.ObjectiveLens(NA=NA, wavelength=532e-9, f0=10, f=1e-3)\n",
    "Ei_fields = objective.getPupilField(z=0, KX=KX, KY=KY, polarization_type=\"linear\",\n",
    "                                    polarization_angle_deg=0)\n",
    "Ej_fields = objective.getPupilField(z=0, KX=KX, KY=KY, polarization_type=\"linear\",\n",
    "                                    polarization_angle_deg=90)  # Field with rotated polarization"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d85adc20-9469-4f36-b177-86e53ac3c093",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### 4. Calculate the BLS signal"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 5,
   "id": "52ac0ad3-d476-40c8-ac88-a79a42229f70",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Calculating BLS signal...\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "print(\"Calculating BLS signal...\")\n",
    "# Using the optimized einsum-based function\n",
    "sigmaSW, qmEiEj = SWT.bls.get_signal_RT_pupil(\n",
    "    KxKy=(kx_grid, ky_grid),  # common k-space grid for E fields and susceptibility\n",
    "    Ei_fields=Ei_fields,\n",
    "    Ej_fields=Ej_fields,\n",
    "    Chi=chi,\n",
    "    coherent_exc=False  # incoherent (thermal) SWs\n",
    ")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "1b9d2112-ff1b-437b-a32b-ed71613e181c",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### 5. Visualize the results"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 6,
   "id": "48bd12b1-dd43-4a8c-ba71-4743e8146378",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 600x400 with 1 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))\n",
    "plt.plot(w_common/(np.pi*2e9), sigmaSW / np.max(sigmaSW))\n",
    "plt.xlabel(\"Frequency (GHz)\")\n",
    "plt.ylabel(\"Normalized BLS intensity\")\n",
    "plt.title(\"BLS spectrum (reciprocity theorem)\")\n",
    "plt.show()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d76628e1-1b26-4843-ad8a-a11ed8e3b051",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### (6. - optional) Visualize the dynamic susceptibility and the transfer function "
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 7,
   "id": "5354d287-dc08-4a27-a9ce-7e398083699f",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 1200x1000 with 18 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    },
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 1200x1000 with 18 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "# Choose frequency to visualize\n",
    "f_desired = 8 # GHz, change this to any frequency you want\n",
    "\n",
    "# Find the closest index in w_common\n",
    "f_idx = np.argmin(np.abs(w_common/(2*np.pi*1e9) - f_desired))\n",
    "f_plot = w_common[f_idx] / (2*np.pi*1e9)\n",
    "\n",
    "fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 3, figsize=(12, 10))\n",
    "plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.4, hspace=0.4)\n",
    "\n",
    "for u in range(3):\n",
    "    for v in range(3):\n",
    "        ax = axes[u, v]\n",
    "        im = ax.imshow(\n",
    "            np.abs(chi[u, v, f_idx, :, :]).T,\n",
    "            extent=(kx_grid[0]*1e-6, kx_grid[-1]*1e-6,\n",
    "                    ky_grid[0]*1e-6, ky_grid[-1]*1e-6),\n",
    "            origin='lower', aspect='auto', cmap='inferno'\n",
    "        )\n",
    "        fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax)\n",
    "        ax.set_xlabel(\"kx (rad/µm)\")\n",
    "        ax.set_ylabel(\"ky (rad/µm)\")\n",
    "\n",
    "plt.suptitle(f\"Dynamic susceptibility tensor χ(u,v) at {f_plot:.2f} GHz\")\n",
    "plt.show()\n",
    "\n",
    "# Plot qmEiEj matrix\n",
    "fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 3, figsize=(12, 10))\n",
    "plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.4, hspace=0.4)\n",
    "\n",
    "for u in range(3):\n",
    "    for v in range(3):\n",
    "        ax = axes[u, v]\n",
    "        im = ax.imshow(\n",
    "            np.abs(qmEiEj[u, v]).T,\n",
    "            extent=(kx_grid[0]*1e-6, kx_grid[-1]*1e-6,\n",
    "                    ky_grid[0]*1e-6, ky_grid[-1]*1e-6),\n",
    "            origin='lower', aspect='auto', cmap='inferno'\n",
    "        )\n",
    "        fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax)\n",
    "        ax.set_xlabel(\"kx (rad/µm)\")\n",
    "        ax.set_ylabel(\"ky (rad/µm)\")\n",
    "\n",
    "plt.suptitle(\"Transfer Matrix (qmEiEj)\")\n",
    "plt.show()"
   ]
  }
 ],
